coredata.dc:title: Cerium-doped carbon dots as dual-target agents against Alzheimer's β-amyloid fibrillogenesis and reactive oxygen species

coredata.dc:description: Both fibrillogenesis of amyloid β-protein (Aβ) and elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Beyond Aβ aggregation inhibition, the complexity necessitates the development of comprehensive therapeutic interventions for halting AD progression. Herein, a dual-target agent capable of Aβ aggregation inhibition and ROS scavenging was synthesized by doping cerium into carbon dots (Ce CDs). Ce CDs with a high Ce (III)/Ce (IV) ratio of 0.67 can scavenge various ROS, including superoxide anion radicals, hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and Aβ40-induced ROS, thus mitigating cellular oxidative damage and rescuing cell viability. Additionally, Ce CDs present potent inhibition on Aβ40 on-pathway fibrillization, disrupting the formation of highly ordered β-sheet structures and increasing cell viability from 50.2 % to 91.9 %. It is validated that the electrostatic interactions between Ce CDs and Aβ40 are primarily responsible for preventing the conformational transition of Aβ40 monomers. In vivo experiments with the transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans strain further validate the bifunctionality of Ce CDs in suppression of Aβ fibrillogenesis and attenuation of oxidative stress, thereby demonstrating the potential of combination therapy for AD. This finding highlights the important role of electrostatic interactions between Aβ and inhibitors in regulating Aβ aggregation, and provides new insights into the development of multifunctional agents for AD treatment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs and Ce CDs. TEM images of (a) CDs and (b) Ce CDs, with inset showing a high-resolution TEM image and particle size distribution, (c) ζ potentials, (d) fluorescence spectra comparison of CDs and Ce CDs, (e) XPS spectra of CDs and Ce CDs, (f) C1s spectra, (g) N1s spectra, (h) O1s spectra, (i) Ce 3d XPS spectrum.

The scavenging effect on ROS. The ability of CDs and Ce CDs to scavenge (a) ·OH, (b) O2 - and (c) H2O2. The significance levels for the pairs of data sets are indicated as follows: * for p < 0.05, * * for p < 0.01, and * ** for p < 0.001. (d) The scavenging ability of CDs and Ce CDs at a concentration of 200 μg/mL for ROS induced by H2O2 (200 μM) and Aβ40 (25 μM) in SH-SY5Y cells. Blue fluorescence (Hoechst 33342) marks the cell nucleus, while green fluorescence (DCF) indicates ROS. Scale bar, 100 μm.

. Detoxification of CDs/Ce CDs on H2O2-induced cytotoxicity to SH-SY5Y cells. (a) Calcein/PI double staining experiment. The concentrations of CDs and Ce CDs were both 200 μg/mL, while H2O2 was at 200 μM. Scale bar is 200 μm. (b) MTT assays. Cell viability treated with PBS alone was set to 100 % (control)·H2O2 concentration was 200 μM. Values of p < 0.05 and p < 0.01 for the pairs of data sets are marked with * and * *, respectively.

Inhibition of Aβ fibrillization. Effects of (a) CD and (b) Ce CDs on Aβ40 aggregation. (c) Relative ThT fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 aggregated with varying CDs or Ce CDs concentrations after 48 h incubation at 37 ℃. #p < 0.05, ##p < 0.01, and ###p < 0.001 as compared to Aβ40 only-treated group. Values of p < 0.001 for the pairs of data sets are marked with * ** . (d) Size distributions of Aβ40 incubated without or with CDs/Ce CDs for 48 h. (e) TEM images of Aβ40 species after incubation for 48 h at 37 ℃.

Detoxification effect of Ce CDs on Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity in SH-SY5Y cells. (a) Calcein/PI double staining experiment. The concentrations of CDs and Ce CDs were both 600 μg/mL, while Aβ40 was at 25 μM. Scale bar is 200 μm. (b) MTT assays. Cell viability treated with PBS alone was set to 100 % (control). Aβ40 concentration was 25 μM. Values of p < 0.05 and p < 0.01 for the pairs of data sets are marked with * and * *, respectively.

Ce CDs attenuated Aβ deposition and associated toxicity in a C. elegans model of AD. (a) Wild-type (N2) nematodes at the L4 larval stage were cultured on NGM plates containing PBS for 3 days and stained by ThT. CL2006 transgenic nematodes at the L4 stage were subjected to three treatment conditions: (b) PBS, (c) CDs, or (d) Ce-CDs for 3 days. ThT was employed to visualize Aβ aggregates in whole-mount nematode preparations following incubation. Scale bar: 50 μm. (e) ROS level of nematodes. (f) Survival curves of N2 and CL2006 AD nematodes under different treatment.

Analysis of the modulation effect of Ce CDs against Aβ40 aggregation. (a) Aggregation kinetics of Aβ40 incubated in the absence and presence of Ce CDs with 100 mM phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at different NaCl concentration conditions. (b) Schematic illustration of the electrostatic interactions between Aβ and Ce CDs. The strengthened electrostatic interactions interfere with the formation of highly ordered β-sheet structures.

. Hemolysis rate of (a) CDs and (b) Ce CDs. (c) Schematic representation of CDs/Ce CDs cross the in vitro BBB model. (d) Microscopy image of the brain endothelial cells tightly grown on the transwell membrane. (e) Quantitative analysis of CDs and Ce CDs transport efficiency. Values of p < 0.05, p < 0.01 and p < 0.001 for the pairs of data sets are marked with * , * * and * ** , respectively. (f) The biodistribution of Cy5-labeled Ce CDs in mice.

Schematic illustration of the preparation and applications of Ce CDs. Ce CDs are synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The ultrasmall Ce CDs can penetrate blood-brain barrier, inhibit Aβ aggregation and regulate oxidative stress.

head.ce:title: Cerium-doped carbon dots as dual-target agents against Alzheimer's β-amyloid fibrillogenesis and reactive oxygen species

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Both fibrillogenesis of amyloid β-protein (Aβ) and elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) contribute to the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease (AD). Beyond Aβ aggregation inhibition, the complexity necessitates the development of comprehensive therapeutic interventions for halting AD progression. Herein, a dual-target agent capable of Aβ aggregation inhibition and ROS scavenging was synthesized by doping cerium into carbon dots (Ce CDs). Ce CDs with a high Ce (III)/Ce (IV) ratio of 0.67 can scavenge various ROS, including superoxide anion radicals, hydroxyl radicals, hydrogen peroxide, and Aβ40-induced ROS, thus mitigating cellular oxidative damage and rescuing cell viability. Additionally, Ce CDs present potent inhibition on Aβ40 on-pathway fibrillization, disrupting the formation of highly ordered β-sheet structures and increasing cell viability from 50.2 % to 91.9 %. It is validated that the electrostatic interactions between Ce CDs and Aβ40 are primarily responsible for preventing the conformational transition of Aβ40 monomers. In vivo experiments with the transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans strain further validate the bifunctionality of Ce CDs in suppression of Aβ fibrillogenesis and attenuation of oxidative stress, thereby demonstrating the potential of combination therapy for AD. This finding highlights the important role of electrostatic interactions between Aβ and inhibitors in regulating Aβ aggregation, and provides new insights into the development of multifunctional agents for AD treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer’s disease (AD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that accounts for more than 78 % of dementia cases among the elderly [1,2]. It is clinically characterized by a gradual decline in memory and cognitive functions. Currently, over 55 million people worldwide are affected by dementia, with AD being the most common form of dementia, potentially leading to 60–70 % of cases [3]. Despite advances in treatment, current medications only partially alleviate cognitive disorders and fail to effectively halt the progression, leading to incurable and relentless situations [4]. Regarding the pathogenesis of AD, several hypotheses have been proposed, with the amyloid cascade hypothesis gaining generally acceptance [5–7]. This hypothesis posits that the aggregation and accumulation of extracellular amyloid β-protein (Aβ) from soluble monomers to oligomers and amyloid plaques is a key event in the development of AD pathology [8,9]. Therefore, development of anti-amyloid agents to inhibit Aβ aggregation is believed to be an promising strategy for AD therapy.

Great achievements have been made in the design of Aβ inhibitors for regulation of Aβ aggregation, including organic molecules [10–12], peptides [13,14], and nanoparticles [15–17]. Carbon dots (CDs), as a type of zero-dimensional carbon material, have attracted widespread attention because of their excellent solubility, ease of functionalization, high specific surface, and good biocompatibility [18,19]. Additionally, the small size of CDs allows them to easily cross blood-brain barrier (BBB), which is critical for modulation of Aβ fibrillization that deep-seated in the cerebrum [20]. CDs with unique chemical compositions, such as amino groups, nitro groups, pyrrole rings, pyrimidine rings, and benzene rings, can interact with Aβ species through various types of interactions, including electrostatic interactions, H-bonding, and hydrophobic interactions, thereby disrupting the Aβ aggregation [21,22]. However, the pathogenic complexity of AD makes Aβ-targeted drugs including CDs unable to reverse AD progression. Besides, elevated levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS) can cause oxidative stress, DNA damage, and neuronal cell death, representing another therapeutic target of AD patients [23]. Dysfunctional mitochondria and Fenton-like reactions involving metal ions and Aβ species may produce excessive ROS, potentially exacerbating the progression of AD [5]. Therefore, it is imperative to design a dual-functional modulator with Aβ aggregation inhibition and ROS scavenging capabilities.

Common drugs that can scavenge ROS include natural antioxidants, such as epigallocatechin gallate, shikonin, quercetin, lycopene, and grape seed extract, as well as natural enzymes like superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase [24–28]. However, poor stability and limited antioxidant capacity hinder the application of the natural polyphenolic and protein-based antioxidants. These limitations have prompted the development of various nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics, namely nanozymes [29,30]. Recently, various artificial nanozymes capable of scavenging ROS have been developed as innovative and effective approaches for the treatment of ROS-related diseases [31]. Among them, ceria nanoparticles are widely studied as alternatives to natural enzymes due to their excellent catalytic activities [32,33]. The unique interconversion between Ce (III) and Ce (IV) oxidation states enables ceria-based materials to scavenge various ROS, such as hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide (·O2 −), making them potential antioxidants [34,35]. Importantly, the auto-catalytic properties of nanoceria allow them to provide sustained antioxidant effects, showing promising application prospects in oxidative-related diseases [35,36].

To endow CDs with antioxidant property and enhance the inhibitory effects of CDs, herein, we proposed to synthesize cerium-doped CDs (Ce CDs) using a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method ( Scheme 1). The prepared Ce CDs were comprehensively evaluated for their potential in penetrating the blood-brain barrier (BBB), inhibiting Aβ aggregation, and alleviating oxidative stress by scavenging ROS. Furthermore, the inhibitory mechanism of Ce CDs was analyzed, which would provide deep insights for the design of multifunctional agents against AD.

Thioflavin T (ThT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. O-phenylenediamine (OPD), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), quinine sulfate, Ce (NO3)3·6 H2O, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH; 1 % w/w, aq) were provided by Macklin (Shanghai, China). Aβ40 (>95 %, lyophilized powder) was obtained from Synpeptide (Nanjing, China). 2’,7’-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) and superoxide radical’s detection kit (WST-8) were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Sulfo-cyanine5-NHS ester was received from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium/Ham’s Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM/F12), fetal bovine serum (FBS), human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells and brain capillary endothelial cells (bEnd.3) were obtained from Pricella Biotechnology (Wuhan, China). All other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and were sourced locally without additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs and Ce CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and Ce CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. For CDs, OPD (0.4326 g) and glycine (0.6005 g) were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water, then the solution was transferred to 50 mL polytetrafluoron-lined reactor and heated at 200 ℃ for 10 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was dialyzed against deionized water with a dialysis bag (MWCO 500–1000) for 2 days. The CDs were obtained by freeze-drying after filtration with 0.22 μm filter membrane. To prepare Ce CDs, 0.8684 g Ce (NO3)3·6 H2O was dissolved with OPD (0.4326 g) and glycine (0.6005 g) in 20 mL deionized water. The subsequent preparation and purification processes for Ce CDs were similar to those used for the CDs.

The Cy5-labelled Ce CDs were synthesized by mixing Ce CDs and sulfo-cyanine5-NHS ester in PBS buffer (pH 7.4) for 24 h, dialyzing against deionized water for 2 days and freeze-drying with a vacuum freeze dryer (HXLG-10–50D, China).

The morphology and size distribution of CDs and Ce CDs were analyzed using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (F200, JEOL, Japan). X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted with a X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic Al Kα. The ζ potential of CDs and Ce CDs was measured using a Nano Zetasizer (BeNano 90 Zeta, Bettersize, China).The fluorescence spectra of CDs and Ce CDs were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (F2700, HITACHI, Japan).

SH-SY5Y cells were cultured in DMEM/F12 with 10 % (v/v) FBS containing 1 % penicillin and streptomycin. bEnd.3 cells were cultured in DMEM medium with 10 % (v/v) FBS, 1 % penicillin and streptomycin. Cells were incubated in an incubator (Thermo Fisher Scientific) containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C and routinely subcultured once every 3 days.

Fresh solution of Aβ40 monomer, ThT, CDs, and Ce CDs were prepared using PBS (pH 7.4). Aβ40 monomers were mixed with ThT in the absence and presence of varying concentrations of CDs/Ce CDs, then incubated at 37 ℃. The final concentrations of Aβ40 monomer and ThT were both 25 μM. The fluorescence intensity of ThT was measured at 480 nm with excitation at 440 nm, and readings were taken every 10 min prior to each measurement. The ThT fluorescence curve was used to characterize the aggregation kinetics of Aβ40, and it was fitted with the following equation [22],
(1)

where y represents the fluorescence intensity at time t, y 0 and y max denote the initial and maximum fluorescence intensities, respectively, t 1/2 indicates the time required for the fluorescence intensity to reach half of its maximum value, and k is the apparent fibril growth rate.

A stock solution of Aβ40 monomer was mixed with CDs or Ce CDs, respectively. The final concentration of Aβ40 was 25 μM. The resulting mixed solution was incubated in a shaker for 48 h at 37 °C and 150 rpm. The Nano Zetasizer mentioned above was use to analyze the size distribution of Aβ40 aggregates. The results were expressed as the average of three three replications.

For the inhibition of aggregation experiment, Aβ40 monomers (25 μM) were incubated without or with CDs/Ce CDs (300 μg/mL) at 37 °C and 150 rpm for 48 h. 10 μL samples of each group were added to the copper mesh for 1 min and then negatively stained with 2 % phosphotungstic acid for 60 s. Grids were observed by a transmission electron microscope (HT7800, HITACHI, Japan).

For the disaggregation experiment, Aβ40 monomers were incubated at 37 °C and 150 rpm for 48 h to obtain Aβ40 aggregates, and then co-incubated with CDs/Ce CDs for another 48 h. The final concentrations of Aβ40 and CDs/Ce CDs were 25 μM and 600 μg/mL, respectively. Then the morphology of each sample was observed by TEM negative staining according to the above method.

Calcein-AM/PI staining assay was performed to identify living and dead cells. SH-SY5Y cells (120 μL) was added into 96-well plate at a density of 15000 cells/well and incubated for 24 h. Then, 30 μL of Aβ40 or co-culture samples of Aβ40 and CDs/Ce CDs were added, which were pre-cultured in a shaker at 37 °C for 6 h. After incubation for another 24 h, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were treated with Calcein-AM/PI dyes for 30 min. The cells were then washed with PBS for three times and observed by fluorescence microscopy. Samples prepared by mixing H2O2 (200 μM) with different concentrations of CDs/Ce CDs were also analyzed by the Calcein-AM/PI staining method.

MTT assay was conducted with bEnd.3 and SHSY-5Y cells as reported previously [37]. Cells (120 μL) was added into 96-well plate (15000 cells/well) and incubated overnight. Samples (30 μL) were added into wells and incubated with cells for 24 h. Then, 10 μL MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS buffer) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. The cells were centrifuged and re-dissolved with 100 µL of dimethyl sulfoxide. The cell viability was determined by measuring the absorbance of each well at 570 nm using a microplate reader. All of the experiments were performed with three replications.

Methylene blue (MB) was employed to determine the amount of ·OH, which was produced by the Fenton reaction between FeSO4 and H2O2 [38]. Freshly prepared MB and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were successively added to CDs or Ce CDs samples with different concentration, followed by adding freshly prepared FeSO4 solution. Sulfuric acid water solution (pH=3) was applied as a solvent and the final concentrations of MB, H2O2 and FeSO4 were 100 μM, 100 μM and 2 mM, respectively. MB was oxidized by the generated ·OH, leading to a decrease in absorbance at 664 nm. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 60 min, the absorbance at 664 nm was recorded by fluorescence spectrometer and the scavenging rate of ·OH was calculated.

A superoxide radical’s detection kit (WST-8) was used to measure the ability of CDs and Ce CDs to scavenge ·O2 − according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Different concentrations of CDs/Ce CDs were mixed with WST-8 working solution, xanthine solution and xanthine oxidase (XO). After the reaction of the mixed solution for 30 min, the absorbance of each sample at 450 nm was detected and the scavenging rate of ·O2 − was calculated.

Firstly, H2O2 (10 mM) was mixed with various concentrations of CDs or Ce CDs for 4 h. Then FeSO4 (1 mM) and terephthalic acid (TA) (45 mM) were rapidly mixed for an additional hour. The remaining H2O2 produces ·OH via Fenton reaction, oxidizing TA to form hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH), which has maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 315 nm and 425 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of each sample were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm. The clearance rate of H2O2 by CDs and Ce CDs was determined by measuring the change in fluorescence intensity of TAOH at 425 nm.

The ability of CDs/Ce CDs to scavenge intracellular ROS was detected by using H2O2 and Aβ40 as ROS inducers, respectively. SH-SY5Y cells were seeded into confocal dishes with 40000 cells per dish and incubated for 24 h. Samples of H2O2 (200 μM) with and without CDs/Ce CDs (200 μg/mL) dissolved in PBS were added into cells, followed by incubation for 3 h. Then culture medium was removed and the cells were stained with mixed dyes (10 μM DCFH-DA and 10 μg/mL Hoechst 33342 in serum-free MEM) for 0.5 h in dark environment. Finally, the cells were washed gently with PBS buffer and observed by confocal microscopy (Leica STELLARIS 5). The intracellular ROS levels induced by Aβ40 (25 μM) were also detected by DCFH-DA probes.

Wild-type N2 and transgenic CL2006 nematodes (Caenorhabditis Genetics Center, USA) were cultured at 20 °C on the nematode growth medium (NGM) (1 mM MgSO4, 1 mM CaCl2, 250 mM KH2PO4, 5 μg/mL cholesterol, 3 g/L NaCl, 2.5 g/L peptone, 17 g/L agar) with E. coli OP50 as food.

ThT staining was performed to visualize the distribution of amyloid plaques in vivo [39]. The L4-stage larvae of CL2006 nematodes were placed on fresh NGM plates containing E. coli OP50 and incubated for 3 days, either without or with CDS/Ce CDS (300 μg/mL, 200 μL). After incubation, the nematodes were fixed in 4.0 % paraformaldehyde at 4 °C for 24 hours and subsequently stained with ThT (25 μM in PBS). The nematodes were observed under an inverted fluorescence microscope, followed by three washes with deionized water. Wild-type N2 nematode was used as the negative control.

The in vivo ROS levels were quantified through DCFH-DA assay with C. elegans [40]. Following 3-day administration of L4-stage nematodes with either PBS buffer or CDs/Ce CDs solution (300 μg/mL, 200 μL), nematodes were harvested and subsequently incubated with 50 μM DCFH-DA (50 μM) for 2 h under light-protected conditions. Then DCF fluorescence was measured with a fluorescence spectrometer. Each treatment group consisted of sixty nematodes.

The lifespan assay was performed as previously [17]. First, 300 μL of fluorodeoxyuridine nucleoside (150 μM) was added to freshly prepared NGM plates for sterilization. Subsequently, L4 larval CL2006 nematodes (50–60 nematodes per plate) were gently transferred to the corresponding plates containing either CDS or Ce CDS (300 μg/mL, 300 μL). Wild-type N2 nematodes were cultured as the negative control. Throughout the experiment, the nematodes on each plate were observed daily, and those that responded to a gentle touch with a platinum wire were counted each day. The survivors from each group were carefully transferred to new plates containing CDS or Ce CDS every 3 days for continued observation and recording.

Mouse red blood cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs or Ce CDs at 37 ℃ for 2 h. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, supernatant was added to a 96-well plate, the absorbance value at 570 nm was measured using a fluorescence reader (SpectraMax M2, Molecular Devices, USA) and quantitative analysis of cell hemolysis was carried out. In this experiment, the hemolysis rate was assessed using water as the standard for complete hemolysis and PBS buffer as the standard for no hemolysis.

Ce CDs (in PBS) were injected into mice at the dose of 20 mg/kg via intravenous injection. The equal amount of PBS was administered as the negative control. On the 14th day after administration, the mice were euthanized, and their tissue samples of heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were collected for hematoxylin and eosin staining.

The bEnd.3 cells were incubated in the upper layer of the transwell plate to establish in vitro BBB model [41]. Following confirmation of BBB integrity via microscopic observation, the culture medium was replaced with PBS (pH 7.4) and CDs/Ce CDs was added to the top chamber. The fluorescence intensities of CD and Ce CDs at 368 nm (excited at 320 nm) were recorded at different time and transport efficiency was estimated according to the fluorescence-concentration standard curve of CDs/Ce CDs.

To investigate the metabolism and biodistribution of Ce CDs, the mice were injected with Cy5-labelled Ce CDs at the dose of 20 mg/kg through the tail vein. The main organs including heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and brain were dissected after 1, 2, 4, and 8 h. Tissue distribution of Ce CDs was recorded by in vivo fluorescence imaging system (In-Vivo Master, China).

Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analyses were conducted by a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey’s test, with p < 0.05 considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Ce CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ce CDs were successfully synthesized through a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method, using Ce (NO3)3·6 H2O as the cerium source and OPD as the carbon source. Meanwhile, CDs were synthesized without adding cerium sources. TEM images show that nearly spherical CDs and Ce CDs were successfully prepared, with average sizes of 2.20 nm and 1.56 nm, respectively, and both exhibit good monodispersity and uniform size ( Figs. 1 a and b). High-resolution TEM images reveal a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite.

The ζ potentials of CDs and Ce CDs in ultrapure water and PBS were measured using a ζ potential analyzer, as illustrated in Fig. 1 c. The ζ potentials of CDs in ultrapure water and PBS were −20.6 mV and −12.1 mV, respectively, suggesting the excellent good stability of CDs. Meanwhile, Ce CDs exhibited a ζ potential of −9.2 mV in PBS, but a positive value of 11.2 mV in ultrapure water. These results indicate that doping with cerium altered the surface charge distribution of CDs, leading to an increase in positive charges on their surface. Fig. 1 d shows that both CDs and Ce CDs have a maximum emission wavelength of 368 nm when excited at 320 nm. Moreover, cerium doping improves the fluorescence performance of the CDs. The fluorescence quantum yields of CDs and Ce CDs were calculated to be 5.56 % and 18.06 % (Table S1), respectively. This is consistent with the literature on heteroatom-doped CDs, as heteroatomic doping in the CDs structure can provide an energy trap state, where photoexcited electrons can be captured, thus avoiding self-quenching [42].

To quantitatively analyze the chemical compositions and states in CDs and Ce CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out. The XPS full scan spectrum of CDs in Fig. 1 e displays three peaks at 284.8, 400.9, and 531.1 eV, which are attributed to carbon (60.85 %), nitrogen (13.05 %), and oxygen (26.10 %), respectively, While Ce CDs show four peaks, demonstrating the presence of carbon (38.44 %), nitrogen (11.98 %), oxygen (45.91 %), and cerium (3.67 %). The high-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra reveal that both CDs and Ce CDs contain C-N, C-O, CC, CO, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N moieties (Figs. 1 f-h). Moreover, compared with CDs, a typical Ce-OH configuration was detected at 532 eV in the high-resolution O1s spectrum of Ce CDs (Fig. 1 h) [32], indicating the doping of Ce. The high-resolution spectrum of Ce 3d (Fig. 1 i) confirms the coexistence of Ce (III) and Ce (IV) in the Ce CDs, as there are two prominent peaks at 886.6 and 905.1 eV that correspond to Ce (III), and four peaks appear at 883.2, 887.8, 901.0, 908.5, and 917.8 eV, indicating the presence of Ce (IV) [43]. The Ce (III) sites are responsible for the elimination of ·O2 − through superoxide dismutase (SOD)-mimetic activity and reduction of ·OH through redox reactions, while Ce (IV) sites are known to oxidize H2O2 by catalase (CAT)-mimetic action [44]. A high proportion of Ce (III) is crucial for treating oxidative stress-related diseases, as ·O2 − and ·OH are directly associated with cell death. By analyzing the characteristic peaks of Ce (III) and Ce (IV) in the Ce 3d spectrum (Fig. 1 i), the ratio of Ce (III)/Ce (IV) in Ce CDs was determined to be 0.67, implying a strong potential to scavenge ROS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ce CDs scavenge ROS and protect cells against oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of CDs and Ce CDs to scavenge ·OH, generated from Fenton reaction between FeSO4 and H2O2, was analyzed by measuring the change in absorbance of MB at 664 nm ( Fig. 2 a). CDs removed 57 % of ·OH at a concentration of 200 μg/mL, likely due to the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on their surface, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl groups. These groups can capture and neutralize ROS, thereby providing an antioxidant effect [45]. After doping with cerium, the scavenging ability of Ce CDs for ·OH was remarkably improved. At a low concentration of 12.5 μg/mL, the ·OH scavenging rate reached 43 %, and at 200 μg/mL, the removal rate of ·OH achieved 67 %, surpassing that of CDs. The scavenging effects of CDs and Ce CDs on ·O2 - were then investigated using xanthine oxidase method [37]. As shown in Fig. 2 b, Ce CDs eliminated 41 % and 68 % of ·O2 - at 100 and 500 μg/mL, respectively, showing a higher scavenging efficiency for ·O2 - compared to CDs, which only removed 23 % of ·O2 - at 500 μg/mL. The high concentration ratio of Ce (III) to Ce (IV) (Fig. 1 i) facilitates the elimination of ·O2 − through SOD-mimetic activity at Ce (III) sites, as well as reduces of ·OH through redox reactions.

Terephthalic acid was used to evaluate the scavenging effect of CDs and Ce CDs on H2O2, and the scavenging rate was calculated from the changes in fluorescence intensity at 425 nm. Both CDs and Ce CDs decreased the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm in a dose-dependent manner, confirming their capability to scavenge H2O2 (Fig. S1). The removal efficiency of Ce CDs for H2O2 is significantly superior to that of CDs. At a concentration of 200 μg/mL, Ce CDs removed 93 % of H2O2, nearly four times more than the scavenging rate of CDs (Fig. 2 c). This result demonstrates that doping with the Ce element significantly enhances the ability of Ce CDs to eliminate H2O2.

Accumulating evidence indicates that Aβ aggregates trigger metal dyshomeostasis and mitochondrial damage, leading to high levels of ROS, progressive oxidative stress and ultimate cell death [46,47]. DCFH-DA, capable of penetrating cells and reacting with intracellular ROS to form the green fluorescent product dichlorofluorescein (DCF), was applied to explore the scavenging ability of CDs and Ce CDs on H2O2 and Aβ40-induced ROS in SH-SY5Y cells. Fig. 2 d shows that after co-incubation of H2O2 or Aβ40 with SH-SY5Y cells, distinct green fluorescence was exhibited, suggesting the formation of a significant amount of ROS. CDs alleviated H2O2- and Aβ40-induced ROS to some extent, but clear green fluorescence remained. In contrast, the addition of Ce CDs significantly decreased the green fluorescence, indicating the enhanced potential of Ce CDs to scavenge intracellular ROS induced by both H2O2 and Aβ40.

The effect of Ce CDs on protecting the cells from oxidative H2O2 damage was evaluated with SH-SY5Y cells through a Calcein-AM/PI double staining experiment ( Fig. 3 a), where live cells were labeled with Calcein-AM, ultimately showing green fluorescence of Calcein, while PI indicates dead cells and shows red fluorescence. The untreated SH-SY5Y cells showed only green fluorescence. Treatment with H2O2 (200 μM) resulted in numerous scattered red fluorescence signals, indicating the cytotoxicity of H2O2. The presence of Ce CDs decreased red fluorescence in H2O2-treated cells more significantly than CDs, confirming the improved effectiveness of Ce CDs in scavenging excess intracellular H2O2 and attenuating the associated cytotoxicity. Consistent with the results of the Calcein-AM/PI double staining experiment, MTT assays further demonstrated that Ce CDs increased the viability of H2O2-treated cells from 47.4 % to 75.3 % at a concentration of 25 μg/mL and to 93.6 % at 400 μg/mL (Fig. 3 b), which is significantly superior over CDs. The enhanced antioxidant properties of Ce CDs contribute to eliminate the high level of ROS, thus mitigating the oxidative damage and cell death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced inhibition of Aβ40 fibrillization via Ce-doping
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Figs. 4 a-c, a ThT fluorescence assay was conducted to quantitatively analyze the β-sheet content of Aβ aggregates that is related with the inhibitory effects of CDs/Ce CDs. The kinetic growth curves were obtained by fitting to (1). Aβ40 alone presented a sigmoidal aggregation curve with a lag, elongation, and plateau phase (Fig. 4 a). Both CDs and Ce CDs decreased the finial fluorescence intensity of Aβ40 in a concentration-dependent manner. CDs exhibited a weak inhibitory effect, resulting in only a 19 % reduction in ThT fluorescence intensity at a high concentration of 600 μg/mL. By contrast, Ce CDs showed enhanced inhibitory potency than that of CDs (Figs. 4 b and c). Ce CDs reduced ThT fluorescence intensity by 27 % at 75 μg/mL and suppressed 84 % of it at 600 μg/mL (Fig. 4 c). In addition, at 300 and 600 μg/mL, Ce CDs slow the aggregation rate of Aβ40, as evidenced by the reduced elongation rate (Fig. 4 b). The ThT experiment demonstrates the potential of Ce CDs to inhibit the aggregation of Aβ40 into highly ordered β-sheet structures.

To further confirm the inhibitory effect of Ce CDs, the size distribution of Aβ40 species was analyzed using DLS assays. Fig. 4 d shows that after 48 h of incubation, Aβ40 formed aggregates with an approximate particle size of 1000 nm. CDs showed limited inhibitory ability. After co-incubation with Aβ40 for 48 h, the size of aggregates ranged from 800 to 1000 nm, which is similar with that of Aβ40 aggregates. In contrast, Ce CDs exhibited a strong inhibitory potency, reducing the size to below 100 nm. As the Ce CDs concentration increased, the size of Aβ40 aggregates gradually decreased, demonstrating a concentration-dependent inhibitory effect. At a high concentration of 600 μg/mL, the size of formed aggregates was less than 10 nm, indicating that Ce CDs can interact with Aβ40 monomers and prevent them from aggregating into large species.

TEM was then employed to observe the morphology of Aβ40 aggregates (Fig. 4 e). It is visualized that Aβ40 formed dense and entangled fibrils after being cultured for 48 h, which is consistent with previous reports [48]. In the presence of 300 μg/mL CDs, distinct fibrous aggregates were observable. By contrast, after incubating Aβ40 with 300 μg/mL of Ce CDs, no visible fibrous aggregates were detected, with only a few small spherical and amorphous structures. The TEM imaging assay further validate that Ce CDs inhibit Aβ40 fibrillization more effectively than CDs, aligning with the results from ThT and DLS assays.

Additionally, TEM images in Fig. S2 show that after co-incubating the pre-formed Aβ40 fibrils with CDs or Ce CDs (600 μg/mL) for 48 h, there were no significant changes in either the length or the number of the fibrils. This suggests that the disaggregation effect of Ce CDs on Aβ40 fibrils was not remarkable.

The effect of Ce CDs on the cytotoxicity of Aβ40 aggregates was evaluated with SH-SY5Y cells through a Calcein-AM/PI double staining experiment ( Fig. 5 a). Only green fluorescence was observed in the control group of SH-SY5Y cells. The introduction of 25 μM Aβ40 results in significant cell death, as indicated by abundant red fluorescence (PI-positive staining). CDs slightly decrease the red fluorescence of dead cells, whereas Ce CDs significantly alleviate the cytotoxicity caused by Aβ40, substantially reducing the number of red fluorescent cells. This result visually indicates the detoxification effect of Ce CDs on Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity.

MTT assay quantitatively showed that Aβ40 aggregates reduced cell viability to 50.2%, and the addition of CDs exhibited no significant changes in viability of Aβ40-treated cells (Fig. 5 b). Notably, Ce CDs elevated the viability of Aβ40-treated cells to 77.9% at 150 μg/mL and to 91.9% at 600 μg/mL, presenting strong detoxification effect. The MTT experiment results demonstrate that Ce CDs can more effectively regulate the transition of Aβ40 to off-pathway aggregation, significantly reducing Aβ40-induced cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of Aβ deposition and toxicity in Caenorhabditis elegans
ce:section.ce:para: C. elegans is widely used as a model for studying AD and other neurodegenerative diseases [39,49]. The transgenic nematode CL2006, which continuously expresses the human Aβ peptides, displayed characteristic age-dependent paralysis and death correlated with Aβ aggregation [50,51]. The levels of amyloid protein deposition in the nematodes were detected using the ThT staining assay. As a control, the wild-type N2 nematodes exhibited only diffuse background fluorescence ( Fig. 6 a). By comparison, the distinct green fluorescent puncta in the CL2006 nematode indicated the accumulation of Aβ aggregates (Fig. 6 b). It is worth noting that after treatment with CDS and Ce CDS, the fluorescent plaques were reduced, with Ce CDS being more effective in inhibiting Aβ deposition in the CL2006 nematodes than CDS, highlighting the enhanced anti-amyloidogenic properties conferred by cerium functionalization (Figs. 6 c and d).

As illustrated in Fig. 6 e, the CL2006 C. elegans strain exhibited significantly elevated intracellular ROS levels compared to wild-type N2 nematodes, due to the pathological overexpression of Aβ and redox imbalance. Treatment with Ce CDs effectively attenuated ROS accumulation in CL2006 nematodes, nearly restoring the levels to those observed in wild-type N2 controls, demonstrating the direct ROS scavenging of Ce CDs via cerium’s redox-active Ce (Ⅲ)/Ce (Ⅳ) cycling.

The dual inhibitory effect of Ce CDs on Aβ fibrillogenesis and oxidative stress were further proved through lifespan assay (Fig. 6 f). Untreated CL2006 nematodes exhibited a shortened lifespan of 12 days, consistent with previously reports [17,39]. Remarkably, Ce CDs treatment extended the lifespan to 16 days, showing a superior detoxification effect than CDs. This longevity rescue strongly validates the synergistic therapeutic effect of Ce CDs by reducing Aβ aggregation (Fig. 6 d) and scavenging ROS (Fig. 6 e).

To explore the possible reason for the improved inhibition of Ce CDs, the structures of Aβ40 and Ce CDs were analyzed. Aβ40 is a hydrophobic peptide with negative net charge (see sequence in Fig. S3) [52]. The central hydrophobic domain (CHD, K16-A21) and C-terminal hydrophobic domain (G29-V40) can facilitate the formation of intermolecular interactions between Aβ monomers, thereby promoting the nucleation and subsequent elongation events [53]. Besides, other domains on Aβ40 are also associated with Aβ fibrillization. For instance, EDVGSNK fragment contains a salt bridge that can stabilize Aβ dimer [54]. The resulting hinge region along with hydrophobic patches can enhance backbone-backbone interactions among Aβ peptides, further contributing to the β-sheet assembly. Therefore, both hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions contribute to the Aβ aggregation, and any disruption of these interactions could potentially affect the on-pathway fibrillization. The cerium doping changes the surface composition of Ce CDs, resulting in a relative positive charges on the surface (Fig. 1 c). It is thus proposed that enhanced electrostatic interactions between Ce CDs and Aβ40 may play a crucial role in preventing the conformational transition of Aβ monomers.

To validate the above mechanism, the aggregation kinetics of Aβ40 incubated in the absence and presence of Ce CDs with different NaCl concentration in PB buffer were analyzed. From Fig. 7 a, as salinity increased, the ThT fluorescence intensities in the Ce CDs-treated groups rose significantly, suggesting a sharp decline in inhibition. At high salt concentration of 600 μM NaCl (high ionic strength environment), where electrostatic effects are largely shielded, Ce CDs showed almost no influence on Aβ aggregation. This result confirms that the interactions between Ce CDs and Aβ40 are primarily driven by strong electrostatic effects (Fig. 7 b), which potentially lead to the disruption of salt bridges and interfere with the formation of highly ordered fibrillar structures. Thus, Ce CDs mainly altered the aggregation pathway of Aβ40 through electrostatic interactions, preventing Aβ from generating toxic amyloid species.

The biocompatibility of CDs and Ce CDs was evaluated in vitro and in vivo. As shown in Figs. 8 a and b, the hemolysis rates for both CDs and Ce CDs were below 2 % even at a concentration as high as 800 μg/mL. Additionally, the viability of cells (bEnd.3 and SH-SY5Y) were maintained over 90 % when treated with either CDs or Ce CDs at 600 μg/mL (Fig. S4). Moreover, in comparison with the control group, H&E staining of main organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of mice with 14-day post-injection of Ce CDs (20 mg/kg) showed no visible damage (Fig. S5). These results indicate that Ce CDs exhibit good biosafety and biocompatibility.

The ability of AD therapeutics to penetrate the BBB is critical for their efficacy. An in vitro BBB model was established with bEnd.3 cells cultured on transwell inserts to simulate the physiological barrier (Fig. 8 c) [55]. After the brain endothelial cells grown on the transwell membrane to form a tight monolayer (Fig. 8 d), the BBB permeability efficiency was calculated according to the fluorescence-concentration standard curve of CDs/Ce CDs (Fig. S6). Fig. 8 e demonstrates that CDs and Ce CDs exhibited good BBB permeability, with rates exceeding 60 % after 5 h of incubation, suggesting their potential to traverse the BBB. The small size of Ce CDs likely allows them to passively diffuse through intercellular spaces or the phospholipid bilayer of cell membranes. To validate these findings in vivo, the distribution of Cy5-labeled Ce CDs in mice was monitored after intravenous administration. Fig. 8 f demonstrates that Ce CDs accumulated in the brain within 2 h post-injection, peaking at this timepoint before gradually declining, which supports their BBB-penetrating capability. The biodistribution analysis revealed that Ce CDs primarily accumulated in the liver, with limited sustained retention in the brain. The signal diminished to baseline levels by 8 h, indicating the rapid systemic clearance and metabolism. Although these results confirm the ability of Ce CDs to cross the BBB, the limited brain-specific targeting and short residence time highlight the need for structural optimization, such as conjugating BBB-targeting ligands [56], to reduce off-target distribution and improve therapeutic efficacy for AD.

In this work, we synthesized cerium-doped CDs using a one-pot hydrothermal method as bifunctional agent targeting Aβ and ROS in AD therapy. Doping with cerium improves the antioxidant properties of Ce CDs, facilitating the effective elimination of ROS such as ·OH, ·O2 −, H2O2, and those induced by Aβ40, thus alleviating cellular oxidative damages and enhancing cell viability. Besides, cerium doping also enhances the positive charge distribution on the surface of Ce CDs, allowing them to primarily bind to Aβ species via electrostatic interactions and inhibit the Aβ fibrillogenesis. In vivo investigations revealed that Ce CDs exhibited pronounced efficacy in reducing Aβ plaque accumulation and attenuating ROS levels in transgenic nematode CL2006, leading to a prolongation of lifespan. In addition, Ce CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, and future optimization of their BBB permeability while minimizing non-target tissue accumulation could substantially enhance their therapeutic efficacy for AD. These findings emphasize the crucial role of the electrostatic interactions between Aβ and inhibitors in regulating Aβ aggregation, offering new insights into the design of multifunctional agents against AD.
